- The Intel architecture suffers from not having enough registers
- Very soon one runs out of registers to use
- Parameter passing by registers is not used as much with larger programs
- Modern architectures use registers more effectively
- **Solution:** Pass parameters on stack

### Method 2: Stack-Based Parameter Passing

Parameters are pushed before the call:

```asm
; Caller
push 5          ; Second parameter
push 3          ; First parameter
call addTwo
add esp, 4      ; Clean up stack (cdecl convention)

; Procedure
addTwo PROC
    push bp
    mov bp, sp
    mov ax, [bp+6]    ; Get first parameter (return address + saved BP)
    add ax, [bp+4]    ; Get second parameter
    pop bp
    ret
addTwo ENDP
```

### Register Preservation

When a procedure needs to use registers, two approaches exist:

**Callee saved:** Procedure clears registers for its own use, restoring them before returning

**Caller saved:** Calling program saves registers it wants preserved

```asm
; Caller saved approach
push ax          ; Save ax before call
push dx          ; Save dx before call
call myproc
pop dx           ; Restore dx
pop ax           ; Restore ax
```

### Important Note on PUSHA/POPA

Either way you may need to use pusha, pushf, popa, popf.

**Note:** MASM generates an error message if using the PUSHA or POPA instructions. By default the MASM generates code for the 8086 processor, and these instructions are implemented only for the 80186, 80286, 80386 processors. To solve this problem, use a .186, .286, or .386 directive in the first line of your code.

See "Error A2105 with PUSHA and POPA instructions"

sections.title: Recursion
sections.content:
## Recursion in Assembly

### What is Recursion?

A procedure may call itself, similar to recursion in C or Java.

**Process:**
1. Procedure call pushes IP register to stack
2. IP is set to beginning of procedure
3. Each return pops the correct address

### Key Challenge

Limited stack space makes recursion tricky in assembly. Each recursive call uses stack space for:
- Return address
- Parameters
- Local variables
- Saved registers

### Example Structure

```asm
recursiveProc PROC
    push bp
    mov bp, sp
    
    ; Check for base case
    cmp [bp+6], 0
    je done
    
    ; Prepare for recursive call
    dec word ptr [bp+6]
    call recursiveProc
    
done:
    pop bp
    ret
recursiveProc ENDP
```

### Recursion and Stack Frames

- The stack can get out of hand if you are using too many recursive functions
- You can access any point in the stack using BP/SS registers
- Stack does not handle dynamic memory allocation - this is handled by the heap (not covered in this course)
- Example: malloc()

> **Tip:** Recursive procedures must have a base case that stops the recursion, and must ensure the stack is properly unwound on each return.

### See Example File

See recurs.asm for a complete example of recursive procedures in assembly.

sections.title: Arrays in Assembly
sections.content:
## Arrays in Assembly

### Defining Arrays

Arrays in assembly are 1-dimensional and are defined in the following ways:

```asm
; 8 element uninitialized array
myArray db 8 dup(?)

; 8 element array, all elements initialized to 1
myArray db 8 dup(1)

; 8 element array with explicit values [0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7]
myArray db 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

; Word array
myArray dw 4245h, FFFFh, 0017h, C642h
; Values: [45h 42h FFh FFh 17h 00h 42h C6h]
```

### Array Address

The effective address of an array is the memory address of the first element.

```asm
; Load effective address of array in dx
LEA DX, myArray
MOV BX, OFFSET myArray    ; Same result
```

Remember: Indexing into memory must be done using registers SI, DI, or BX.

### Indexing Arrays

Using register indirect addressing, we can easily access elements of the array:

```asm
; Move array element 4 to register al
mov al, myArray[4]

; Using register indirect
mov si, 4
mov al, myArray[si]   ; Same result
```

### Memory-to-Memory Operations

```asm
; ILLEGAL - Cannot move memory to memory
mov myArray[2], myArray[5]

; Must use a register
mov al, myArray[5]
mov myArray[2], al
```

> **Important:** The offset [num] is the offset in bytes, regardless of whether the array was defined using db/dw/dq.

### Working with LEA

As with strings, the effective address of the array is the memory address of the first element. The load effective address instruction (lea) can be used in the same way:

```asm
; Compute address of array element
LEA DX, myArray        ; Loads effective address of array in dx
```

sections.title: Reading and Printing Numbers
sections.content:
## Reading and Printing Numbers

### The Challenge

Recall that we were able to read, display, and perform arithmetic with one digit numbers. We concluded that multi-digit numbers would have to be decoded one digit at a time.

**There is no ASCII character for '10', so we process '1' and '0' separately.**

### Steps to Read a Number

1. Read string of digits into buffered input
2. Point to last character in string
3. Convert using ASCII, multiply by a power of 10
4. Store result, check if done
5. Otherwise, point to next character and repeat steps 3 and 4

### Steps to Print a Number

1. Integer division of a large number by 10 gives 'ones' place as remainder
   - Example: 13/10 = 1 + 3R
2. Convert remainder to ASCII and push to stack
3. If quotient is non-zero, repeat steps 2-3 on the quotient
4. Pop chars off the stack one at a time and print (one pop for every division)

### Example: Print 13

```asm
print_int PROC
    mov ax, 13     ; Number to print
    mov cx, 0      ; Digit counter
    
next_digit:
    mov dx, 0
    mov bx, 10
    div bx         ; AX = quotient, DX = remainder
    add dx, '0'    ; Convert to ASCII
    push dx
    inc cx
    cmp ax, 0
    jne next_digit
    
print_loop:
    pop dx
    mov ah, 02h
    int 21h
    loop print_loop
    
    ret
print_int ENDP
```

Let's step through the procedure print_int slowly with the "13" example. This will be a handy procedure for you in future assignments/labs.

sections.title: Extending 8-bit to 16-bit Programs
sections.content:
## Extending 8-bit Programs to 16-bit

### The Challenge

In the last example, multiplication required the use of 3 registers. Space is very limited once data sizes become large.

When data sizes become large, we use the stack to preserve values!

### Using Stack to Preserve Registers

```asm
; Save registers before multiplication
push ax          ; Save ax
push dx          ; Save dx
mul bx           ; Multiply - overwrites ax, dx
...              ; Other instructions
pop dx           ; Restore dx
pop ax           ; Restore ax
```

### Using Data Segment for Storage

Alternatively, reserve space in data segment:

```asm
; Data Segment
temp dw 1 dup(?)    ; Reserve a word (2 bytes)

; Code Segment
mov temp, dx        ; Save dx in temp variable
mul bx              ; Multiplication overwrites ax, dx
...
mov dx, temp        ; Restore dx from temp
```

### Comparing Approaches

| Method | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| Stack | No extra memory needed | Stack management complexity |
| Data segment | Clear intent | Uses data memory |

> **Tip:** The stack approach is generally preferred for short-term preservation, while data segment variables are better for values that need to persist across many operations.

sections.title: Floating Point in Assembly
sections.content:
## Floating Point in Assembly

### Overview

In this course we have only introduced integer and bit-wise instructions. We will not implement any floating point arithmetic in assembly in this course.

Instead, we will briefly overview the hardware components of the x86 architecture responsible for floating point operations.

### x87 Floating Point Hardware

Original x86 processors had a separate coprocessor for FP arithmetic called x87 chips.

### FP Registers

Floating point registers are **80-bits wide** (long double size):

```
ST0
ST1   <- Stack organization
ST2      (ST0-ST7)
ST3
ST4
ST5
ST6
ST7
```

### Key Points

- General purpose registers cannot be used for floating point operations
- Special floating point instructions push and pop operands to/from the x87 register stack
- Most instructions take the first couple stack elements as input by default
- The debugger cannot view x87 registers directly - must pop x87 registers to data/stack segment and view by dumping memory

### Basic FP Instructions

| Instruction | Operation |
|------------|-----------|
| `FADD op` | ST[0] = ST[0] + op (op can be memory or x87 reg) |
| `FADDP op` | Same as above, but pop the x87 stack after |
| `FSUB op` | ST[0] = ST[0] - op (op can be memory or x87 reg) |
| `FSUBP op` | Same as above, but pop the x87 stack after |
| `FLD op` | Load ST[0] with op (32- or 64-bit operand from mem) |
| `FST op` | Move data from ST[0] to op (32- or 64-bit operand) |
| `FSTP op` | Same as above but pop the x87 stack after |

> For full instruction set, see: www.idc-online.com/technical_references/pdfs/electronic_engineering/Data_Types_and_Instruction_Set_of_8087.pdf

sections.title: MOVSB and MOVSW - Move String
sections.content:
## MOVSB and MOVSW - Move String Bytes/Words

The `MOVSB` (Move String Byte) and `MOVSW` (Move String Word) instructions copy data from one memory location to another.

### How They Work

- **MOVSB**: Copies one byte from [DS:SI] to [ES:DI]
- **MOVSW**: Copies one word from [DS:SI] to [ES:DI]

### Registers Used

| Register | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| **SI** | Source Index (DS:SI) |
| **DI** | Destination Index (ES:DI) |
| **CX** | Counter (used with REP prefix) |
| **DF** | Direction Flag (controls increment/decrement) |

### Examples

```asm
; Copy 10 bytes from source to dest
LEA SI, source      ; SI = offset of source
LEA DI, dest        ; DI = offset of dest
MOV CX, 10          ; Counter = 10
REP MOVSB           ; Copy 10 bytes

; Using MOVSW for words
LEA SI, word_src
LEA DI, word_dst
MOV CX, 5           ; Copy 5 words
REP MOVSW           ; Copy 5 words (10 bytes)
```

> **Important:** The direction flag (DF) determines if SI/DI are incremented or decremented after each copy.

sections.title: LODSB and LODSW - Load String
sections.content:
## LODSB and LODSW - Load String Byte/Word

The `LODSB` and `LODSW` instructions load a byte or word from [DS:SI] into AL or AX.

### How They Work

- **LODSB**: AL = [DS:SI], then SI is incremented/decremented
- **LODSW**: AX = [DS:SI], then SI is incremented/decremented by 2

### Key Characteristics

- Used for reading from a source string into a register
- Typically used in loops when you need to process each element
- Direction flag (DF) controls whether SI is incremented or decremented

### Example: Process Each Character

```asm
LEA SI, myString    ; SI points to string
MOV CX, 10          ; String length = 10

next_char:
    LODSB           ; AL = [DS:SI], SI updated
    ; Process character in AL
    CMP AL, 'A'     ; Compare to 'A'
    JL not_upper    ; Jump if less
    CMP AL, 'Z'     ; Compare to 'Z'
    JG not_upper    ; Jump if greater
    ; AL is uppercase letter
not_upper:
    LOOP next_char
```

> **Tip:** LODSB/LODSW are typically used when you need to examine or modify each data element one at a time.

sections.title: STOSB and STOSW - Store String
sections.content:
## STOSB and STOSW - Store String Byte/Word

The `STOSB` and `STOSW` instructions store a byte or word from AL or AX into [ES:DI].

### How They Work

- **STOSB**: [ES:DI] = AL, then DI is incremented/decremented
- **STOSW**: [ES:DI] = AX, then DI is incremented/decremented by 2

### Key Characteristics

- Used for writing a value to every element in a destination string
- Often used with REP prefix to fill memory with a value
- ES must be set to the appropriate segment

### Example: Fill Memory with Value

```asm
LEA DI, buffer      ; DI = destination
MOV AX, 'AA'        ; Value to store (two bytes)
MOV CX, 100         ; Fill 100 words
REP STOSW           ; Fill memory with 'AA'
```

### Example: Building a String

```asm
LEA DI, output      ; DI = output buffer
MOV AL, 'H'         ; First character
STOSB               ; Store 'H'
MOV AL, 'i'         ; Second character
STOSB               ; Store 'i'
MOV AL, '!'         ; Third character
STOSB               ; Store '!'
; DI now points past the last character
```

> **Tip:** STOSB/STOSW with REP is excellent for initializing arrays or clearing buffers.

sections.title: REP Prefixes - Repetition
sections.content:
## REP Prefixes - Repetition

The REP prefix repeats a string instruction CX times.

### REP Family

| Prefix | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `REP` | Repeat while CX != 0 (for MOVS, STOS, LODS) |
| `REPE` / `REPZ` | Repeat while CX != 0 AND ZF = 1 |
| `REPNE` / `REPNZ` | Repeat while CX != 0 AND ZF = 0 |

### How REP Works

```asm
MOV CX, 10         ; Repeat 10 times
REP MOVSB          ; Copy 10 bytes
```

Execution:
1. Check if CX = 0, if yes exit
2. Execute the string instruction
3. Decrement CX
4. Repeat

### REPE/REPNE for Searching

These prefixes compare ZF after each iteration:

```asm
; Find first non-zero byte
MOV CX, 100        ; Search up to 100 bytes
LEA DI, buffer     ; Buffer to search
MOV AL, 0          ; Value to find
REPNE SCASB        ; Search while [DI] != AL
; When done: DI points to first non-zero byte or CX = 0
```

### Common Patterns

```asm
; Copy string (null-terminated)
MOV CX, 0FFFFh     ; Max length
LEA SI, src
LEA DI, dest
REP MOVSB          ; Copy bytes

; Fill buffer with zeros
MOV CX, 100
LEA DI, buffer
XOR AX, AX         ; AX = 0
REP STOSB          ; Fill with zeros

; Compare two strings
MOV CX, 10
LEA SI, str1
LEA DI, str2
REPE CMPSB         ; Compare while equal
; If ZF = 1 after loop, strings are equal
```

> **Important:** Always set CX to the correct count before using REP!

sections.title: CMPSB - Compare String Bytes
sections.content:
## CMPSB and CMPSW - Compare String

The `CMPSB` (Compare String Bytes) and `CMPSW` (Compare String Words) compare data at [DS:SI] with [ES:DI].

### How They Work

- **CMPSB**: Compares [DS:SI] with [ES:DI], sets flags
- **CMPSW**: Compares word at [DS:SI] with word at [ES:DI]

### Flags After Comparison

| Result | ZF |
|--------|-----|
| [SI] == [DI] | 1 |
| [SI] < [DI] (unsigned) | 0, CF = 1 |
| [SI] > [DI] (unsigned) | 0, CF = 0 |

### With REPE/REPZ

Use with REPE to find equal strings:

```asm
; Check if two 10-byte strings are equal
MOV CX, 10
LEA SI, str1
LEA DI, str2
REPE CMPSB
; After loop:
;   If CX = 0 and ZF = 1: Strings are equal
;   If CX > 0 and ZF = 0: First mismatch at position (10 - CX)
```

### Example: String Comparison

```asm
compare_strings PROC
    PUSH CX
    MOV CX, 0          ; Count matching characters
    
compare_loop:
    MOV AL, [SI]       ; Get char from str1
    CMP AL, [DI]       ; Compare with str2
    JNE done           ; Not equal, exit
    INC SI
    INC DI
    INC CX
    CMP CX, 10         ; Max length
    JL compare_loop
    
done:
    POP CX
    RET
compare_strings ENDP
```

> **Tip:** CMPSB sets flags like SUB but doesn't modify either operand - perfect for comparisons without destroying data.

sections.title: SCASB and SCASW - Scan String
sections.content:
## SCASB and SCASW - Scan String

The `SCASB` and `SCASW` instructions compare AL or AX with memory at [ES:DI].

### How They Work

- **SCASB**: Compares AL with [ES:DI]
- **SCASW**: Compares AX with word at [ES:DI]

### Purpose

Scan a string to find a specific value (like searching for a character in a buffer).

### With REPNE/REPNZ

Use with REPNE to find first matching/non-matching value:

```asm
; Find first 'A' in string
MOV CX, 100         ; Max search length
LEA DI, buffer      ; Buffer to search
MOV AL, 'A'         ; Character to find
REPNE SCASB         ; Search while [DI] != AL
; After loop:
;   If CX = 0: Reached max length without finding
;   If CX > 0: DI points to first 'A' (or CX decremented past it)
```

### Example: Find Null Terminator

```asm
; Find end of null-terminated string
MOV CX, 0FFFFh      ; Max length
LEA DI, myString    ; String to scan
XOR AL, AL          ; AL = 0 (null character)
REPNE SCASB         ; Search for null
; DI now points past the null (or at max)
DEC DI              ; Back to null position
```

### Example: Count Characters

```asm
; Count 'X' characters in string
MOV CX, 100
LEA DI, buffer
MOV AL, 'X'
XOR DX, DX          ; Counter = 0

count_loop:
    SCASB           ; Compare AL with [DI], update DI
    JNE next        ; Not equal
    INC DX          ; Found one!
next:
    LOOP count_loop
; DX = count of 'X'
```

> **Tip:** SCASB/SCASW with REPNE is the fastest way to search for a byte in a buffer.

sections.title: CLD and STD - Direction Flag
sections.content:
## CLD and STD - Direction Flag Control

The Direction Flag (DF) controls whether string instructions increment or decrement the index registers.

### How DF Affects String Instructions

| DF | SI/DI Behavior |
|----|-----------------|
| **DF = 0 (CLD)** | Increment (left to right) |
| **DF = 1 (STD)** | Decrement (right to left) |

### Instructions

| Instruction | Effect |
|------------|--------|
| `CLD` | Clear DF (increment mode) |
| `STD` | Set DF (decrement mode) |

### Default State

On program start, DF = 0 (increment mode). Always set DF explicitly before string operations to ensure correct behavior.

### Example: Forward Copy (DF = 0)

```asm
CLD                  ; DF = 0 (increment)
LEA SI, source       ; SI = start of source
LEA DI, dest         ; DI = start of destination
MOV CX, 10
REP MOVSB            ; Copy from low to high addresses
```

### Example: Backward Copy (DF = 1)

```asm
STD                  ; DF = 1 (decrement)
LEA SI, source[9]   ; SI = end of source
LEA DI, dest[9]     ; DI = end of destination
MOV CX, 10
REP MOVSB            ; Copy from high to low addresses
```

### When to Use Each Mode

| Mode | Use Case |
|------|----------|
| CLD (DF=0) | Normal string processing, left-to-right traversal |
| STD (DF=1) | Backward processing, building strings in reverse |

> **Critical:** Always set DF before string operations! If DF is in the wrong state, string operations will copy data in the wrong direction.

sections.title: Complete String Processing Examples
sections.content:
## Complete String Processing Examples

### Example 1: Copy Null-Terminated String

```asm
; Copy string (stops at null terminator)
COPY_STRING PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH SI
    PUSH DI
    
    MOV CX, 0FFFFh    ; Max length
    CLD               ; Increment DI and SI
    
copy_loop:
    LODSB             ; Load from source
    STOSB             ; Store to destination
    CMP AL, 0         ; Check for null
    JE copy_done
    LOOP copy_loop
    
copy_done:
    POP DI
    POP SI
    POP CX
    RET
COPY_STRING ENDP
```

### Example 2: Uppercase a String

```asm
; Convert string to uppercase
TO_UPPER PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH SI
    MOV CX, 100
    CLD
    
upper_loop:
    LODSB             ; Get character
    CMP AL, 'a'       ; Check if lowercase
    JB skip           ; Less than 'a'
    CMP AL, 'z'       ; Check if lowercase
    JA skip           ; Greater than 'z'
    SUB AL, 20h       ; Convert to uppercase
skip:
    ; AL already processed
    LOOP upper_loop
    
    POP SI
    POP CX
    RET
TO_UPPER ENDP
```

### Example 3: Find Character in String

```asm
; Find character in string, return position or -1
FIND_CHAR PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DI
    MOV CX, 100
    CLD
    REPNE SCASB       ; Search for AL
    JZ found          ; ZF = 1 means found
    MOV AX, -1         ; Not found
    JMP done
found:
    MOV AX, 100
    SUB AX, CX         ; Position = original CX - remaining CX
done:
    POP DI
    POP CX
    RET
FIND_CHAR ENDP
```

### Example 4: String Comparison

```asm
; Compare two strings, return 0 if equal
COMPARE_STRINGS PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH SI
    PUSH DI
    MOV CX, 100
    CLD
    REPE CMPSB         ; Compare while equal
    MOV AX, 0          ; Assume equal
    JZ done            ; If ZF = 1, completely equal
    MOV AX, -1         ; Not equal
done:
    POP DI
    POP SI
    POP CX
    RET
COMPARE_STRINGS ENDP
```

> **Tip:** Always save registers that you use in string procedures and restore them before returning.

sections.title: Storage Technology Trends and Locality
sections.content:
## Storage Technology Trends and Locality

### Recall: Storage Technology Trends

**Observations:**
- CPU speed is out-pacing all storage
- SRAM is keeping up, but DRAM and Disk are sorely lacking
- SRAM is too expensive to use for main memory
- DRAM and disk are main storage devices
- Creates the CPU-memory gap: CPU is limited by speed of memory and disk
- To bridge gap: modern systems use SRAM based caches
- This approach works because most programs exhibit good locality

## Locality

### What is Locality?

Well written programs tend to reference data items that:
- Are near to other recently referenced items
- Were recently referenced themselves

### Two Forms of Locality

**Spatial Locality:** If a memory location was referenced, memory locations nearby will likely be or have been referenced.

**Temporal Locality:** If a memory location was referenced, the memory location is likely to be referenced again in the near future.

### Why Consider Locality?

Programs with good locality will run faster than programs with poor locality. All levels of a computer system exploit locality:

- **Hardware:** Uses caches to speed up memory accesses
- **Operating Systems:**
  - Main memory is used as a cache for the most recently used chunks of the virtual address space
  - Use main memory as cache for disk data
- **Applications:** Web browser cache recently viewed pages on disk

### Principle of Locality

Two different things are fetched from memory during execution:
- **Program data:** Fetched at request of program
- **Program instructions:** Fetched at request of CPU

Locality principles apply to both.

### Locality of References to Program Data

Consider a function like maxvec. The reference pattern for each variable:

**Max:**
- Scalar: no spatial locality
- Accessed at least once in each loop iteration: good temporal locality

**i:**
- Scalar: no spatial locality
- Accessed at least once in each loop iteration: good temporal locality

**v:**
- Array elements are accessed sequentially: good spatial locality
- Each element is accessed once: bad temporal locality

Since all variables have either good temporal or spatial locality, maxvec has good locality. Maxvec accesses elements sequentially: said to have a stride of 1.

**Definition:** stride-k means function visits every kth item. Stride-1 is most common and has good spatial locality. Greater the stride, the worse the locality.

### Locality of Instruction Fetches

- CPU fetches instructions from memory in sequential order unless a branch occurs
- These are also memory accesses
- Small tight loops enjoy both good spatial and temporal locality
- Large functions enjoy good spatial locality, bad temporal locality
- Small functions that are not called from loops enjoy neither

### Summary of Locality

- Programs that repeatedly reference the same variables enjoy good temporal locality
- For program with k-stride patterns, the smaller the stride the better the spatial locality
- Loops have good temporal and spatial locality
- Smaller loops have better spatial locality
- More loop iterations have better temporal locality

sections.title: The Memory Hierarchy
sections.content:
## The Memory Hierarchy

### Two Fundamental Properties

1. Different storage technologies have widely ranging access times
2. Faster technologies are more expensive and have less capacity

Well written programs tend to have good locality. These principles complement each other and suggests organization of computer memory into a hierarchy.

### Memory Hierarchy Organization

In general:
- Storage devices get slower, cheaper, and larger as we move from higher to lower levels

**At the highest level (L0):** A small number of fast CPU registers
- Accessed in a single clock cycle

**Next:** One or more small SRAM-based cache memories
- Accessed in a few clock cycles

**Followed by:** DRAM-based memory
- Accessed in 10s to 100s clock cycles

**Next:** Slow enormous disks

### Memory Hierarchy Levels

| Level | Device | Holds | Retrieved from |
|-------|--------|-------|----------------|
| L0 | Registers | Words retrieved from L1 cache | CPU registers |
| L1 | On-chip cache | Cache lines retrieved from L2 | L2 cache |
| L2 | Off-chip cache | Lines retrieved from main memory | Memory |
| L3 | Main Memory | Disk blocks retrieved from local disk | Local disks |
| L4 | Local Disks | Files retrieved from remote sources | Remote storage |
| L5 | Remote secondary storage | End of hierarchy | - |

### Caching in the Memory Hierarchy

A cache is a small fast storage device that acts as a staging area for data objects in slower devices.

**Caching:** The process of using a cache

**Main idea:**
- Faster & smaller memory at level k acts as a cache for larger & slower memory at level k+1
- Each level "caches" data objects from the next lower level

### Block Organization

- Storage at level k+1 is partitioned into blocks
- Each block has a unique address
- Blocks can be either fixed (in most cases) or variable size

Storage at level k is partitioned into smaller set of same-sized blocks. Data is copied between levels k and k+1 in units corresponding to the size of the block. Note: different block sizes between different levels.

**General principle:** Lower in hierarchy = longer access and larger blocks.

### Cache Hits and Misses

**When a program looks for data object d at level k+1:**
- It first looks for d at level k
- If d is cached at level k, then this is called a cache hit
- Program reads d from level k, which is faster than level k+1

**If d is not found (cache miss):**
- Cache at level k fetches block d from level k+1
- If level k cache is full, fetched block overwrites another block
- The overwritten block is called the victim block
- The victim block is said to be evicted from the cache
- Method used to perform eviction is called the replacement policy:
  - Random
  - Least Recently Used (LRU)

Once d is read into level k, it can be used by the program.

### Three Types of Cache Misses

1. **Compulsory misses:** Caused by an empty cache (cold cache)
2. **Conflict misses:** Could have been avoided had the cache not evicted an entry earlier
3. **Capacity misses:** Misses that occur solely due to finite size of the caches

When a block is loaded into cache, it must have a place. Ideal: a flexible policy to place block anywhere in cache.

**Problem:** Caches at top of hierarchy must be fast, such a policy would be too expensive to implement in hardware.

**Solution:** Hardware caches restrict where blocks can be placed to a subset or even singleton of blocks at level k. Example: block i can be placed only in location i mod 4.

However: Even if cache is not full, another block may have to be evicted.

**Working Set:** Programs generally work in phases. In each stage, program accesses a limited # of blocks. This set of blocks is called the working set. If working set fits in cache, great! Program runs quickly. If working set does not fit, program wastes time evicting and replacing blocks in cache.

### Cache Management

At each level of memory something must manage the cache (evict and load blocks, decide which blocks to replace):
- **Compiler** manages L0
- **Hardware** manages L1/L2
- **Hardware/OS** manages L3
- **OS** manages L4 (many disks also have a hardware cache)

### Summary of Memory Concepts

**Exploiting Temporal Locality:**
- Objects will be accessed many times
- First time object is loaded into cache
- In the future object is accessed from the cache faster

**Exploiting Spatial Locality:**
- Blocks contain multiple data objects
- First object causes block to be loaded into cache
- Next object accessed after first object will already be in the cache

sections.title: Cache Memories - General Operation
sections.content:
## Cache Memories - General Operation

### General Cache Operation

Assume the following memory hierarchy: Registers, L1 cache, Memory.

### Generic Cache Structure

The cache must determine whether a request is a hit or a miss, and extract the requested word. This process consists of three steps:

1. **Set selection:** Use s bits of address to identify which set the block may be cached in
2. **Line matching:** Check if valid bit is set AND if tag bits match
3. **Word selection:** Use block offset to select the word from the block

### Address Division

CPU requests word at address A. A is divided into three parts:
- **Tag:** Used to determine which line, if any, the block is in
- **Set:** Used to determine which set the block may be cached in
- **Offset:** Offset of word in block

Cache uses s bits of A to identify the set that may contain the block. Cache uses tag and valid bit to determine if a line in set contains the block. Offset bits are used to load word if found. Otherwise cache loads block from memory.

### Types of Cache

Caches are grouped into different classes based on E (# of cache lines per set):
- **Direct-mapped caches:** Easiest to understand and implement
- **Set associative caches:** Hard to implement
- **Fully associative caches:** Hard to implement

### Direct-Mapped Cache

**Key characteristic:** Each set has 1 line (E = 1). Therefore # of sets = # of lines.

**DM: Set Selection**
To determine if cache contains word at address A: Find set using s bits of A to index into array of sets.

**DM: Line Matching**
- Check if valid bit is set
- Check if tag bits of A match tag of line
- If above conditions are true: cache hit
- Otherwise: miss, then load block from memory, replace line with block, set bit to valid, extract word in block

**DM: Word Selection**
When a hit occurs (or block was loaded from memory), we know word is somewhere in the block. The block offset provides us with the offset of the first byte in the desired word. Think of block as an array of bytes, and the byte offset as an index into that array.

### Example (pg. 619-621)

The mechanisms that a cache uses to select sets and identify lines are extremely simple. Have to be, because hardware must perform them in a few nanoseconds. However, manipulating these bits can be confusing to us.

**Given:** (S,E,B,m) = (4, 1, 2, 4)

**Accessing address 8:**
- tag = 1, set = 0, block offset = 0
- Cache checks set 0 for a tag of 1. If found, byte at offset 0 is read (cache hit)
- Otherwise, update the cache: M[8], M[9] are the bytes of data located at addresses 8, 9

**Now try to access address 0:**
- tag = 0, set = 0, block offset = 0
- Currently cache at set 0 shows tag of 1. We wanted tag of 0, so cache miss
- Evict the line and update the cache: M[0], M[1] are the bytes of data located at addresses 0, 1

### Direct Mapped Cache - Advantages and Disadvantages

**Advantages:**
- Very fast - code to determine if set contains block is very simple

**Disadvantages:**
- Each set can only hold one line
- Results in thrashing

**Example of thrashing:** Two blocks map to the same set. Program accesses each block in alternating order. Each time block is accessed, other block is evicted from cache. Cache is forced to reload block on each access. Slows down program execution significantly (as much as 2x or 3x). Also, conflict misses stem from the constraint that each set has exactly one line.

sections.title: Set Associative and Fully Associative Caches
sections.content:
## Set Associative and Fully Associative Caches

### Set Associative Caches

**Key characteristic:** 1 < E < C/B. Called E-way set associative caches. Each set contains multiple lines.

**SA: Set Selection**
Identical to direct-mapped caches: s bits identify the set.

**SA: Line Matching and Word Selection**
Check ALL lines in set in parallel.

Retrieve word if line is valid and if line contains matching tag. Otherwise, load block from memory (assuming only 1 cache). If no empty lines are available (all lines are valid), then evict a line from the set. Use offset to get word in cached block.

### Line Replacement

Which line to evict?

**Replacement policy:** Method to select block for eviction
- **Random:** Choose a line at random from the set
- **LFU:** Least frequently used
- **LRU:** Least recently used

First policy is cheap, but results in more conflict misses. Latter policies are more expensive, but result in less conflict misses.

### Caching Example

General: Same procedure as previous example but now we have 2 lines of space for each cache set. If both lines are full, choose which line to evict based on a replacement policy. Replacement policy will vary depending on the storage medium.

### Fully Associative Caches

**Key Characteristic:** E = C/B (S = 1). Cache is a single set with C/B lines. Address is divided into tag and offset. No s bits.

Analogous to a huge hash table.

**Properties:**
- Valid bit and Tag for each line
- Entire cache = 1 set with E = C/B lines in the one and only set

**Works similar to set associative caches:**
- There is only one set
- Check all lines in set (in parallel)
- Retrieve word if line is valid and line contains matching tag
- Otherwise: Choose empty line to place block, Or evict a block if there are no empty lines
- Load block from memory (assuming only 1 cache)
- Use offset to get word in cached block

### FA: Line Matching and Word Selection

Three conditions must all be true for a cache hit:
1. The valid bit must be set
2. The tag bits in one of the cache lines must match the tag bits in the address
3. If (1) and (2), then cache hit, and block offset selects starting byte

### Cache Implementation Considerations

Logic for searching for tags is slow and expensive. Only an option in caches at lower end of hierarchy. Too slow for L1 and L2 cache. L1 and L2 caches use either:
- Direct mapped caches
- 2-way caches
- 3-way caches
- 4-way caches

sections.title: Cache Writes and Performance
sections.content:
## Cache Writes and Performance

### Caches and Memory Writes

**What about writing to memory?**

Recall read procedure: CPU requests word from cache. If block with word is cached, it's a hit. Else it's a miss, and cache fetches block from next level. Word from block is returned once block is cached.

**Writes are more complicated.** Scenario: CPU writes a word to memory. Either block with word is in cache, or not.

**If block is in cache (cache hit):**
- Block in cache is updated with word
- Eventually memory has to be updated with word
- What does cache do about updating the copy of w in the next lower level in the hierarchy?

### Write-Through vs Write-Back

**Two options:**

**Write-through:** Immediately write block to memory
- Simplest to implement
- Increases number of bus transactions

**Write-back:** Defer block write until block is evicted
- Advantage: significantly reduces number of bus transactions
- Disadvantage: additional complexity
- Cache must maintain a 'dirty bit' to keep track of which blocks must be written back when evicted
- Loading cache may take longer because eviction is more complex

### Write Allocation

**Scenario:** CPU writes a word to memory. Either block with word is in cache, or not. If block is not in cache (cache miss) - should the block be loaded?

**Two options:**

**Write-allocate:** Update cache only
- Exploits spatial locality of writes
- Reduces # of bus transactions
- Generally done by write-back caches
- Requires more cache hardware

**No-write allocate:** Send update only to lower level
- More bus transactions will occur
- Cache updated only on hits
- Generally done by write-through caches
- Takes less hardware

### Types of Caches

In many cases CPUs use two caches:
- **d-cache:** For program data - Should handle a wide variety of access patterns - Handles reads/writes
- **i-cache:** For program instructions - Mainly needs to handle simple sequential access - Does not need to handle writes - Can be made simpler and faster than a d-cache
- **Unified cache:** A single cache is used for both instructions and data

Modern processors include separate i-caches and d-caches:
- Processor can read an instruction word and a data word at the same time
- I-caches are typically read-only (simpler)
- Each cache is often optimized to different access patterns
- Different block sizes, associativities, and capacities

**Cache hierarchy for Intel Core i7 processor:**
- Each CPU has four cores
- Each core has its own private L1 i-cache and L1 d-cache
- Each core also has its own L2 unified cache
- All of the cores share an on-chip L3 unified cache
- Note: all SRAM cache memories are contained on the chip

### Performance Impact

Cache performance is evaluated using several metrics:
- **Miss rate:** Fraction of memory references that are cache misses = # misses / # references
- **Hit rate:** Fraction of memory references that are cache hits = 1 – miss rate
- **Hit time:** Time to deliver a cached word to the CPU including time for set selection, line identification, and word selection - several cycles for L1
- **Miss penalty:** Additional time required due to a miss - 10 cycles to load from L2, 40 cycles to load from L3, 100 cycles to load from memory

### Impact of Cache Parameters

**Cache parameters:**
- Cache size: # of bytes cache can store
- Block size: # of bytes stored in a line
- Associativity: # of lines per set

**Impact of cache size:**
- Adv.: Large caches tend to increase hit rate
- Disadv.: Large caches tend to increase hit time (especially important for L1 caches that must have short hit time)

**Impact of block size:**
- Large blocks can increase hit rate if spatial locality is good
- Large blocks imply smaller number of cache lines (C = SxExB)
- Reduction in hit rate in programs with good temporal locality
- Large blocks increase miss penalty (time to load blocks) since larger blocks cause larger transfer times
- Modern systems usually compromise: blocks that contain 32 to 64 bytes

**Impact of associativity:**
- Advantage of higher associativity: reduces thrashing due to conflict misses
- Disadvantages:
  - Slower and more expensive to implement
  - Hard to make fast
  - Requires more tag bits
  - More bits to keep track of which block to evict next
  - Can increase hit time because of increased complexity
  - Can increase miss penalty because of increased complexity of choosing a victim
- Essentially a trade-off between cost, hit-rate, and miss penalty

**Write-through caches:**
- Simpler to implement
- Can use a write buffer that works independently of cache to update memory
- Read misses are less expensive (do not trigger a memory write)

**Write-back caches:**
- Result in fewer transfers
- Allows more bandwidth to memory for I/O devices
- Reducing the # of transfers becomes important as we move down the hierarchy
- In general, caches further down the hierarchy are more likely to use write-back caches

sections.title: The Memory Mountain
sections.content:
## The Memory Mountain

### Overview

Every computer has a unique memory mountain that characterizes the capabilities of the memory system. It shows read throughput as a function of:
- Working set size (temporal locality)
- Stride (spatial locality)

### Memory Mountain for Intel Core i7

The next slide shows the memory mountain for an Intel Core i7 system:
- L1 cache: 32KB
- L2 cache: 256KB
- L3 cache: 8MB
- Working set: size varies from 2 KB – 64 MB, stride varies from 1 to 64 elements

### Reading the Mountain

**Geography reveals a rich structure:**
- Perpendicular to the size axis are four ridges
- Correspond to regions of temporal locality (working set fits entirely in a cache level)
- Note: order of magnitude difference between top of L1 ridge and bottom of memory ridge
- Reads at 6 GB/s vs 600 MB/s

**Slopes of spatial locality:**
- For L2, L3, and main memory ridges there is a slope that falls downhill as stride increases
- Increase in stride = decrease in locality
- Notice even when the working set is too large to fit in any of the caches, the highest point on the main memory ridge is a factor of 7 higher than its lowest point
- Even with poor temporal locality, spatial locality can still come to the rescue

### Prefetching

Notice flat ridge for stride 1. Read throughput is relatively constant at 4.5 GB/s. Due to prefetching mechanism in the Core i7 memory system that automatically identifies memory referencing patterns and attempts to fetch those blocks into cache before they are accessed. Yet another reason to favor sequential access in your code.

### Impact of Cache Size and Temporal Locality

Let's take a slice of mountain holding stride constant to see impact of cache size and temporal locality on performance.

**Up to 32 KB:** Working set fits entirely in L1 d-cache. Thus, reads are served at the peak throughput (6 GB/s).

**Up to 256 KB:** Working set fits entirely in L2 cache.

**Up to 8 MB:** Working sets fits entirely in L3 cache.

**Larger working sets:** Served from memory.

Notice that read throughputs drop when the working sets are equal to their respective cache sizes. Likely drops are caused by other data and code blocks that make it impossible to fit the entire array in the cache.

### Impact of Spatial Locality

Let's take a slice of the mountain holding working set size constant to see impact of spatial locality on read throughput. Let's use a fixed size of 4 MB (cut along L3 ridge). Working set fits entirely in L3 cache (too large for L2 cache).

Notice read throughput decreases steadily as the stride increases from 1 to 8. In this region a read miss in L2 causes a block to be transferred from L3 to L2, followed by some number of hits on the block loaded into L2. Depends on the stride. As the stride increases, the ratio of L2 hits to L2 misses increases. Since misses are slower than hits, the read throughput decreases. Once stride reaches 8, every read request misses in L2.

### Summary: Optimizing for Memory Performance

**Performance of the memory system is not characterized by a single number.** Instead, it is a mountain of temporal and spatial locality. Elevations can vary by over an order of magnitude. Wise programmers try to structure their programs so that they run in the peaks instead of the valleys.

**Goals:**
- Exploit temporal locality so that heavily used words are fetched from L1 cache
- Exploit spatial locality so that as many words as possible are accessed from a single L1 cache

**Broken record:**
- Focus your attention on inner loops (bulk of computations and memory accesses)
- Try to maximize spatial locality by reading objects with stride 1
- Try to maximize temporal locality by using a data object as often as possible once it has been read from memory

sections.title: Cache Types and Modern Processor Cache Hierarchies
sections.content:
## Cache Types and Modern Processor Cache Hierarchies

### Separate I-Cache and D-Cache

Modern processors include separate i-caches and d-caches for important reasons:

**Why Separate Caches?**
- Processor can read an instruction word and a data word at the same time
- I-caches are typically read-only (simpler)
- Each cache can be optimized to different access patterns
- Different block sizes, associativities, and capacities

**I-Cache Characteristics:**
- Mainly needs to handle simple sequential access
- Does not need to handle writes
- Can be made simpler and faster than a d-cache

**D-Cache Characteristics:**
- Should handle a wide variety of access patterns
- Handles reads/writes
- More complex to optimize

### Intel Core i7 Cache Hierarchy

The cache hierarchy for the Intel Core i7 processor demonstrates modern cache organization:

**Level 0 (L0):** CPU Registers
- Holds words retrieved from L1 cache

**Level 1 (L1):** On-chip L1 caches (separate i-cache and d-cache)
- Holds cache lines retrieved from L2 cache

**Level 2 (L2):** Off-chip L2 unified cache
- Holds lines retrieved from main memory

**Level 3 (L3):** On-chip L3 unified cache (shared)
- All cores share this cache

**Key Observations:**
- All SRAM cache memories are contained on the chip
- Each CPU has four cores
- Each core has its own private L1 i-cache and L1 d-cache
- Each core also has its own private L2 unified cache
- All of the cores share an on-chip L3 unified cache

### Cache Parameters Summary

Modern cache design involves trade-offs between:
- **Capacity:** More capacity = better hit rate but longer hit time
- **Block Size:** Larger blocks = better spatial locality but higher miss penalty
- **Associativity:** Higher associativity = less thrashing but slower

The goal is to balance cost, hit rate, and miss penalty for each level of the hierarchy.

sections.title: Processes and the Operating System
sections.content:
## Processes and the Operating System

### What is an Operating System?

The OS is a program (or programs) that:
- Runs on the same CPU
- Uses the same memory
- Accesses the I/O devices
- Is the guardian of the computer

### Roles of the Operating System

1. **Manage and run all programs** - Allow programs to share the CPU
2. **Manage computer resources** - Processor, memory, hard disk, network connection, and I/O devices
3. **Protect processes from each other**
4. **Present a standard abstraction** to programs and users
5. **Act as intermediary** between the programs, user, and computer

### OS Startup and Operation

- The OS is the first program loaded by the computer
- Resides in memory at all times
- Runs when:
  - A program needs a service (performing I/O, run a program, stop running, allocation of more resources)
  - An external event occurs
  - A program error occurs
  - A computer error occurs

### Control Flow Fundamentals

Recall what the processor does:
- The processor executes one instruction after another
- The PC points to the next instruction to execute
- PC takes on a sequence of values a0, a1, a2, … Where each ak is the address of a corresponding instruction Ik
- Each transition from ak to ak+1 is called a control transfer
- CPU control is transferred from instruction at ak to one at ak+1
- A sequence of control transfers is called the flow of control

### Program Control Flow

Transfers are either **smooth** or **abrupt**:
- **Smooth:** Instruction Ik+1 is adjacent to instruction Ik in memory
- **Abrupt:** Ik+1 is not adjacent to instruction Ik in memory
  - Occurs during: Jump or branch, Procedure call, Procedure return

Abrupt control flow transfers are necessary to:
- Allow a program to respond to changes of internal states
- Execute conditional code (if statements)
- Execute repetitive code (loops)
- Use methods and procedures

### Observations

Program has illusion that it is the only thing running on the computer. However, system must react to events outside of the program:
- Keystrokes
- Internet packets
- Mouse movement
- Inter-program signals

How is this done?

sections.title: Exceptional Control Flow
sections.content:
## Exceptional Control Flow (ECF)

### What is ECF?

System reacts to these events by special abrupt changes in control flow called **exceptional control flow (ECF)**.

These occur at all levels of a system:
- **Hardware:** Devices can interrupt current CPU execution via exception handlers
- **Operating system:** Can transfer control between programs via context switches
- **Programs:** Can abruptly change flow of other programs via signals and software exceptions

### Why Study ECF?

We want to study ECF in order to understand how:
- An OS works (basic mechanism)
- Programs (applications) interact with the OS
- To write more interesting applications
- Software exceptions work

### What is an Exception?

An exception is an abrupt change of flow control implemented jointly by hardware and OS.

**When an exception occurs:**
1. Application program is executing instruction Icurr
2. Event occurs here
3. Exception processing begins
4. Exception handler runs
5. Exception return (optional) - returns to either Icurr or Inext

### How Exceptions Work

1. CPU is executing instruction Icurr
2. A significant change in processor's state occurs (called an event)
   - Examples: Divide by 0, Virtual memory page fault, Arithmetic overflow, I/O request completes, Hardware timer
3. Processor detects change
4. Processor responds by making an indirect procedure call through a lookup table called the **exception table**
5. The procedure is an operating system subroutine called the **exception handler**
6. After handler terminates, one of three things happen:
   - Handler returns control to instruction Icurr
   - Handler returns control to instruction Inext
   - Handler aborts program

### Exception Table

- Located in memory
- Created and initialized when OS boots
- Entry k contains the address of the handler for exception k
- Address of table is stored in CPU register called **Exception Table Base Register**
- CPU uses table to locate exception handler

When exception occurs (CPU detects an event):
1. CPU determines ID of exception
2. Uses ID to index into exception table
3. Locates reference to corresponding handler
4. Uses reference to make a procedure call to handler

### Observations About Exception Calls

This call differs from regular procedure call in several ways:
- Return address pushed on stack may be either current or next instruction, depending on type of exception
- Processor also pushes additional information on stack (e.g., the flags register)
- Exception handler has complete control over the system
- The handler then handles the event

sections.title: Classes of Exceptions
sections.content:
## Classes of Exceptions

### Four Classes of Exceptions

1. **Interrupts:** Caused by normal hardware events (asynchronous)
2. **Traps:** Caused by program service requests (intentional)
3. **Faults:** Caused by repairable program "errors"
4. **Aborts:** Caused by irreparable program errors and hardware errors

### Interrupts (Asynchronous)

- Occur due to hardware events: mouse movements, keystrokes, I/O completions
- Not caused by execution of an instruction
- Handlers for interrupts are called interrupt handlers

**Interrupt Handling Sequence:**
1. Interrupt pin goes high during execution of current instruction
2. A hardware device signals a pin on the CPU, at the same time places exception ID on the system bus
3. CPU finishes current instruction
4. CPU reads ID from system bus, invokes handler to handle the device
5. Control passes to handler after current instruction finishes
6. Interrupt handler runs
7. Handler returns to next instruction (Inext)
8. Program continues to execute as if interrupt did not occur

### Traps (Intentional - System Calls)

- Intentional exceptions called a "system call"
- Occur as a result of executing a special "trap" instruction
- Used to request services:
  - Read/write data from/to a file
  - Create a new process (run program)
  - Terminate program
- Provides a procedure-like interface between OS and application
- From a programmer's perspective call looks just like a function call, except:
  - Regular functions run in "user" mode
  - System calls run in "kernel" mode

### Trap Handling

1. Application makes a system call
2. Control passes to handler
3. Trap handler runs
4. Handler returns to instruction following the syscall

### Faults (Possibly Recoverable)

- Result in (possibly) correctable error conditions
- If handler can correct condition, current instruction is retried
- Otherwise program is aborted
- Examples:
  - Page fault: when memory needed by program has been swapped to disk
  - Protection fault: when program references unowned memory

### Fault Handling

1. Current instruction causes a fault
2. CPU identifies fault, assigns ID
3. Uses ID to find handler in exception table
4. Invokes handler
5. If handler corrects fault, handler returns to instruction that caused it
6. Else handler invokes abort routine

### Aborts (Unrecoverable)

- Result from unrecoverable errors: Bad memory, CPU errors
- Same as a fault, but never returns - simply aborts the process

### Abort Handling

1. Fatal hardware error occurs
2. Control passes to handler
3. Abort handler runs
4. Handler returns to abort routine (never returns to program)

### Exceptions on Intel Processors

- Exception table has 256 entries
- Exceptions 0…31 are hardware defined (interrupts and faults)
- A trap is performed by the instruction **int n** where n is a constant between 0 and 255
- To return from an exception handler, use the special **iret** (instead of ret)
- The iret pops the FLAGS register before returning with the return address

sections.title: Processes
sections.content:
## Processes

### What is a Process?

A process is an instance of a program in execution. The idea: Abstraction presents two illusions to a program:

1. **Exclusive use of the CPU:** Program instructions are executed without interruption
2. **Exclusive use of memory:** Program code and data are only objects in memory

**Definition:** A process is an instance of a program in execution.

### Process Context

A process defines the context in which the program runs:
- Program code and data
- Logical control flow of the program
- Private address space
- Contents of general purpose registers
- Program counter

### Process Control Block (PCB)

- Information about a process is stored in a Process Control Block (PCB)
- Allocated by the OS when a process is created
- Is only accessible to the OS
- Used to store parts of the process context

Each time a program is run, the OS:
1. Creates a new context
2. Loads the program code and initial data into context
3. Initializes context to begin execution of program

### Logical Control Flow

If we used a debugger to step through our program, we could trace the contents of our IP register (program counter or PC). This corresponds to addresses of instructions contained in our .exe file, and any other objects linked to program. This sequence is the **logical control flow**.

This sequence of PC values is known as the logical control flow. We can divide a physical control flow into logical control flows. Each logical flow is independent of any other flow. Different processes do not affect the states of any other processes.

### Concurrency

**Concurrent process:** Any process whose logical flow overlaps with another flow. The notion of processes taking turns is called **multitasking**. Each time period that a process executes is called a **time slice**.

Each process executes a portion of its flow and then is pre-empted. Process A & B and A & C run concurrently, but B and C do not (they take turns).

### Parallel Flows

Notice that the idea of concurrency is independent of the number of cores. If two flows overlap, they are concurrent, even if they are running on the same processor.

Sometimes we will find it useful to identify truly "concurrent" flows. Called **parallel flows**: Two flows running concurrently on different processor cores or processors. These flows are said to be running in parallel (have parallel execution).

### Private Address Space

Each process has illusion of exclusive use of system's address space. In actuality, a process provides each program with its own private address space. Private address spaces have similar layouts: Code, data, heap, stack, kernel space.

**Why do we need this abstraction?** To multitask.

sections.title: Context Switching
sections.content:
## Context Switching

### User and Kernel Mode

Private address spaces must remain private! Need to restrict user capabilities. Extra mode bit in a control register usually characterizes privileges of processor. The mode bit sets the process in user or kernel mode.

**Cannot do the following in user mode:**
- Halt processor
- Change mode bit
- Initiate I/O operation
- Directly reference code in kernel area of address space

All programs initially begin in user mode and use exceptions to pass into kernel mode.

### Multitasking

In the past: Computer ran a program, waited until program completed, then ran the next one.

Today: We want many programs to run at once. No program should interfere with another. Programmers should not have to worry what other programs are doing.

**Key Ideas:**
- Each program has its own logical flow, runs as if it is the only program on the computer
- Physically, many programs take turns running on the CPU
- The OS switches between the logical flows
- Creates the illusion that all programs are running at once
- In reality, the processes run concurrently

**To switch processes we simply switch contexts.** Recall, context consists of address space, registers, flags, PC.

**Processes are protected from each other because:**
- Processes can only manipulate data inside their context
- Processes may not manipulate data of another process (context)
- Processes may not manipulate data of the OS
- OS also runs in its own context
- A process need not worry about other processes because it is "alone"

**Question:** When are processes switched in/out?

### When Do Context Switches Occur?

Context switches occur only when an exception occurs:
- Trap
- Interrupt
- Fault

**What if no exceptions occur?** Computer has a timer chip that generates a "timer" exception at fixed intervals (between 10 to 100ms). Exceptions are guaranteed to occur at regular intervals.

### How Context Switching Works

During an exception, the OS:
1. Switches out current process context
2. Switches in OS context
3. Handles the exception (OS code)
4. Selects a process to run (could be the same one)
5. Switches out OS context
6. Switches in the process's context

Switch out/in is called a context switch.

### What's in a Context Switch?

The context switch involves two contexts (out-going and incoming):
1. Save CPU context (state) of current process - Stores all registers (FLAGS and PC included) into PCB (essentially a sequence of mov instructions)
2. Determine PCB of process/OS to be switched in
3. Switch memory address spaces (from the PCB)
4. Load CPU context (state) from current PCB
5. Load all registers FLAGS and PC from PCB
6. Run the switched process

These steps are performed twice for a single exception!

### Scheduling

Decision of kernel to pre-empt a process is called **scheduling**. Handled by part of the kernel called the scheduler.

**Why would a kernel pre-empt a process?**
- Example: Read call in process A requires disk access
- Next instruction must be stalled until information is retrieved
- Kernel decides to switch context for efficiency
- Programs can opt to sleep (request for context to be switched)
- Gets around any scheduling errors

### User and Kernel Modes Revisited

When an exception occurs:
- Control is passed to handler
- CPU is placed in kernel mode
- The handler now has access to OS memory, including PCBs
- Can manipulate OS memory, perform context switch
- When handler returns to process, CPU is placed back in user mode

sections.title: Virtual Memory
sections.content:
## Virtual Memory

### Why Virtual Memory?

Processes in a system share CPU and memory, which poses some challenges:
- Threshold in # of processes, above which some may not be able to run
- Memory is vulnerable to corruption
- Process could write to another process's memory
- Cause the process to fail in some odd fashion unrelated to the program logic

**Solution:** Modern systems provide an abstraction of main memory known as virtual memory.

### Virtual Memory Capabilities

Virtual memory is the interaction of hardware exceptions, hardware address translation, main memory, disk files, and kernel software. It provides three important capabilities:

1. **Cache:** Treats memory as a cache for an address space stored on disk
2. **Simplified management:** Provides each process with a uniform address space
3. **Protection:** Protects address space of each process from corruption by other processes

### Why Study Virtual Memory?

**Virtual memory is central:**
- Encompasses all levels of computer system
- Helps you better understand system in general

**Virtual memory is powerful:**
- Gives applications capabilities to create and destroy chunks of memory
- Map chunks of memory to portions of disk files
- Share memory with other processes
- Reading/writing virtual memory can modify contents of disk
- Can load contents into memory without any explicit copying

**Virtual memory is dangerous:**
- If used improperly, applications can suffer from perplexing and insidious memory related bugs
- Crash minutes or hours after problem occurred in the code
- Run to completion with incorrect results

### Physical Addressing

Recall: Computer main memory is an array of byte-sized cells (CPU point of view). Each byte has a unique physical address (PA).

Most natural way for CPU to access memory is to use physical addresses, called **physical addressing**. Address corresponds to same physical location in memory. The address is from the physical address space. This is the easiest and most natural approach.

**Example:** A load instruction that reads the word at address 4:
- CPU generates physical address
- Sends it over the bus
- Memory fetches four-byte word starting at address 4

### Virtual Addressing

Modern computers have CPU access main memory by generating a virtual address (VA). VA is then converted to PA before being sent to memory. The task of converting a VA to a PA is called **address translation**.

**Requires close cooperation between CPU hardware and the OS:**
- Translation performed by hardware on CPU called the **memory management unit (MMU)**
- Addresses are translated on the fly using a look-up table stored in main memory
- Contents managed by the OS

Called virtual addressing. Each address is virtual from a virtual address space. Translated by MMU. Process thinks it is reading/writing at the specific address. In reality, the read/write occurs at MMU translated address. This allows system to control where the processes read/write memory without process involvement.

sections.title: Page Tables and Paging
sections.content:
## Page Tables and Paging

### Address Spaces

An address space is an ordered set of nonnegative integer addresses {0, 1, 2, …}. Linear address space: If the integers are consecutive. Physical main memory addresses form a linear address space. We will always assume linear address spaces.

**Virtual Address Space:**
- CPU generates virtual address space of N = 2^n addresses {0, 1, 2, …, N – 1}
- Address space size is characterized by number of bits needed to represent largest address
- e.g., virtual address space with N = 2^n addresses is called an n-bit address space
- Modern systems use either 32 or 64-bit virtual address spaces

**Physical Address Space:**
- A system also has a physical address space that corresponds to M bytes of physical memory
- {0, 1, 2, …, M – 1}

**Address space concept is important** because it makes a clean distinction between data objects (bytes) and their attributes (addresses). This allows each byte to have:
- Virtual address from the virtual address space
- Physical address from the physical address space

### VM and Caching

VM is organized as an array of N contiguous byte-sized cells stored on disk (hard drive). Each byte has a unique address (index into array). The contents of the array on disk are cached in main memory.

The data on disk is partitioned into blocks that serve as the transfer units between the disk and the main memory. VM is partitioned into fixed-sized blocks called **virtual pages (VP)**. Each virtual page is P = 2^p bytes in size. Physical memory is partitioned into physical pages (PP). Physical pages are also P bytes in size.

### Virtual Page States

At any point in time, the set of VP is partitioned into three disjoint subsets:

1. **Unallocated:** Pages that have not yet been allocated (or created) by the VM system
   - Contains no data and do not occupy any disk space

2. **Cached:** Allocated pages that are currently cached in physical memory
   - Page contains data
   - Page is assigned to a frame (physical page)

3. **Uncached:** Allocated pages that are not cached in physical memory
   - Page contains data
   - Page is not assigned to a frame (physical page)
   - Page is stored on disk

### Page Tables

**Task:** The MMU has to translate addresses for each running process. For each virtual address determine:
- Which virtual page corresponds to the address
- Whether virtual page is allocated and cached
- Determine location of frame containing the page

To perform the translation, the MMU uses page tables:
- Each process has an associated table
- The tables are stored in physical memory
- Contain page table entries (PTE)

**Page Table Entry (PTE):**
Each PTE contains:
- **Valid bit:** Denotes if the entry is cached or not
- **Address entry:** Frame number if cached, disk address if uncached, or 0 if unallocated

**Address denotes one of three things:**
- If address ≠ 0 AND valid = 1: address contains location of frame (physical page)
- If address ≠ 0 AND valid = 0: address contains location of page on disk
- If address = 0 AND valid = 0: page is unallocated

**Hence a page is:**
- **Unallocated:** if valid = 0, and address = 0
- **Not cached:** if valid = 0, and address ≠ 0
- **Cached:** if valid = 1

### Page Fault Handling

1. MMU issues an exception
2. Invokes page fault handler
3. The handler:
   - Checks if the page is allocated - If not allocated, process is aborted
   - Otherwise, checks for free frame
   - If free frame available, use it
   - If not: Selects a victim page to be evicted (writes to disk if modified)
   - Updates page table entry
   - Uses resulting free frame
   - Loads required page from HD into free frame
   - Updates process' page table
4. Handler returns to faulting process
5. Restarts same instruction that created fault
6. Process proceeds as if nothing has happened

### Page Allocation

Pages are allocated under three conditions:
1. **When a process is created:** Enough pages are allocated to hold code, data, stack, and small heap
2. **When a process needs more heap memory:** Process uses system call to request pages from OS. OS responds by allocating pages for the heap, returns to the process # of pages that were added
3. **When process needs more stack:** OS automatically detects when a process needs more stack, allocates more pages to the stack

When pages are allocated:
- OS modifies corresponding page table entries
- Page is created on disk
- OS may or may not allocate VP to PP mapping

sections.title: Address Translation and TLB
sections.content:
## Address Translation and TLB

### Address Translation

Basic translation: Virtual to Physical. For a given address, the MMU must:
1. Locate the corresponding PTE
2. Determine location of frame that contains the page
3. Compute the offset into the frame (this is the resulting address)

Process is similar to cache. Virtual address is divided into two parts:
- **VPN (Virtual Page Number):** Identifies the page
- **VPO (Virtual Page Offset):** Offset within the page

MMU uses the VPN to locate PTE. Entry in PTE is: physical frame (or page) number. MMU composes PPN (Physical Page Number) and VPO to get physical address.

### Page Hit and Page Miss

**Page Hit:**
- CPU sends address to MMU
- MMU checks if page is in memory
- If page is cached: called a page hit
- MMU translates virtual address into physical address
- Sends it to memory system to read/write data

**Page Miss:**
- If not: called a page miss
- Similar to cache hit/miss
- Page is loaded
- Current instruction retried

### TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)

**Problem:** This process requires accessing more memory (to read PTE).

**Solution:** Use a special cache called **Translation Lookaside Buffer**.

Most programs have good locality:
- i.e., accesses are spatially and temporally localized
- Many accesses will occur within the same set of virtual pages and hence within the same set of physical frames

### TLB Operation

**Idea:**
- Cache PTEs of recently accessed virtual pages in a cache
- Located in the MMU
- Called TLB

**How it works:**
1. Instead of looking up PTE in page table first check TLB
2. If PTE is in TLB, use it
3. Else look in page table
4. Load TLB with new PTE (may need to evict another one)

**TLBs are efficient:** ~95% hit rates because:
- Most programs have good locality
- Many accesses occur within the same set of virtual pages

### TLB Structure

- Translation Lookaside Buffer is used as a cache for page table entries
- Indexing into a TLB is done through tag and index bits
- Very similar to caching concepts from Chapter 6

### Putting It All Together

You should now be able to explain the steps involved in a memory read beginning with an arbitrary virtual address A, ending with an arbitrary DRAM supercell address (i,j).

You should be able to describe the functions of:
- **MMU:** Memory Management Unit - translates virtual addresses to physical addresses
- **Buses:** Transport addresses and data between components
- **I/O controller:** Manages communication with I/O devices
- **Mem/Disk Controllers:** Manage memory and disk operations
- **Caches (On-chip and off):** Speed up memory access
- **Write-through/write-back:** Different cache write policies

**The complete memory read process involves:**
1. Virtual address generated by CPU
2. MMU splits VA into VPN and VPO
3. MMU checks TLB for PTE
4. If TLB miss: look up page table in memory
5. If page fault: load page from disk
6. Once PTE found: translate VPN to PPN
7. Compose PPN with VPO to get physical address
8. Access cache (L1, L2, L3)
9. On miss, access main memory
10. Data returned to CPU

sections.title: Memory Protection
sections.content:
## Memory Protection

### How VM Provides Protection

A useful feature of VM is memory protection. Basic idea:
- Process only sees its own memory
- Hence it can only modify its own memory

**Problem:** Process also "sees" kernel memory in private address space.

**Solution:** Each PTE contains additional permission bits:
- **Read bit:** Can read from page
- **Write bit:** Can write to page
- **Execute bit:** Can execute code in page
- **Supervisor bit:** Kernel mode access only

### Protection Check

Suppose the MMU not only checks if page is resident but also:
- If read operation: whether read bit is set
- If write operation: whether write bit is set
- If CPU in user mode: whether supervisor bit is NOT set

If any of these tests fail, MMU generates a **general protection fault**.

### Why Protection Matters

- Kernel sets permissions on memory accessible by the process
- This prevents process from reading, writing, or executing any memory that is dangerous
- Thus, VM serves as a natural protection system

### Multi-Level Page Tables

PTEs may be multi-level:
- First-level PTE points to second-level page table
- Second-level PTE points to actual frame
- Saves memory when large portions of address space are unused

PTEs are kept separate for processes, even if they share the same physical memory.

### Aside: VM and Caching

Note that valid bit in PTE denotes whether information is cached or not. Page hits and page misses are analogous to cache hits and cache misses from Chapter 6. Waiting until the last minute to swap a page from DRAM to disk is analogous to write-back caches.

### VM and Locality

In the previous examples, we saw how VM consisted of pages from both DRAM and disk. Given disk access times, why isn't VM terribly inefficient?

**Answer:** Locality to the rescue!

Total # of pages probably exceeds total size of physical memory. BUT: Locality promises at at any point in time they will tend to work on smaller set of active pages (working set). See memory mountain - the same principles of temporal and spatial locality that apply to cache also apply to virtual memory.
